steps.tool_result:
{"ok": true, "tool_used": "regex", "suspicious_summary": "# Pre-filtered suspicious candidates (external/public + state-changing + no obvious access-control modifier):
- `initialize` (visibility=external, modifiers=initializer)
- `combinedProposalId` (visibility=public, modifiers=uint72, proposalID)
- `getProposal` (visibility=external, modifiers=Proposal, proposal, bool, hasVoted)
- `deposit` (visibility=external, modifiers=notPaused)
- `depositETH` (visibility=external, modifiers=notPaused)

The ground-truth vulnerable function is highly likely to be in the list above. Use this as your primary candidate pool when ranking top-3."}

tool_args.hypothesis: If the contract is uninitialized, any external caller can call initialize to become DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE and gain admin powers (relayer/resource management), enabling privileged actions.

steps.tool_result: {"ok": true, "target_function": "initialize", "hypothesis": "If the contract is uninitialized, any external caller can call initialize to become DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE and gain admin powers (relayer/resource management), enabling privileged actions."}

tool_args.exploit_logic: Deploy the original QBridge contract in a test, do not call initialize in the test setup, then call initialize from attacker account to set themselves as DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE. Then call an admin-only function (e.g., grant RELAYER_ROLE or set resource handler) to prove admin control. We'll grant RELAYER_ROLE to attacker and assert hasRole(RELAYER_ROLE, attacker).

tool_args.evidence: Foundry test passed: attacker called initialize on uninitialized QBridge and then used admin-only addRelayer to grant RELAYER_ROLE; test asserts hasRole(RELAYER_ROLE, attacker) == true. Passing run proves initialization access-control vulnerability allowing takeover.
